subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recording and processing are generally both clear and precise, with complete tables, units, uncertainties, and error bars
Processed-data table correctly presents means and uncertainties
Theoretical results are calculated with uncertainties and compared visually
weaknesses: Propagation of uncertainty to derived quantities is missing
Some significant omissions in data processing (unjustified high-order fit, inconsistent significant figures)
Consideration of uncertainties shows omissions in fit peak and gradient error analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording is strong enough for the 5â6 band, but consideration of uncertainties and processing quality both exhibit significant omissions. This positions the work in the 3â4 band at the top for a score of 4.

strengths: Clear, focused research question embedded in a specific experimental context
Detailed apparatus list with measurable specifications and reproducible procedure
Comprehensive variables table identifying independent, dependent, and controlled variables with uncertainties
weaknesses: Everyday relevance of the investigation is only briefly touched upon
Some theoretical derivations (flatâcoil vs. infinite solenoid) lack justification
Minor omissions in apparatus specifications (coil thickness, centring method)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (research question context and methodological description) clearly meet the 5â6 band, while consideration of methodological choices is solid but not fully explained quantitatively, placing the work at the top of the 5â6 band for a score of 5.

strengths: Conclusion correctly identifies the fieldâs increase and decrease and notes qualitative agreement with theory
Comparison of experimental and theoretical curves includes percentage errors at each point
Discussion references accepted BiotâSavart context for an IB DP student
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative comparison of key metrics (e.g., peak positions, ÏÂ²)
Discrepancies are only briefly discussed without deeper physics explanations
No hard numerical summary to reinforce consistency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is described and shows relevant comparison to scientific context, but missing quantitative support and deeper justification. This aligns with the 3â4 bandâs top level, meriting a score of 4.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (probe alignment, current stability, environmental field) are identified
Realistic and relevant improvements (higher resolution, isolated lab, Helmholtz coil extension) are described
Insightful extension to Helmholtz coils demonstrates forward thinking
weaknesses: Explanation of the relative impact of each limitation is brief and qualitative
Some suggested improvements lack quantified expected outcomes
Strengths table repeats procedural points and needs fuller articulation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes realistic and relevant improvements and identifies specific methodological limitations, placing the evaluation in the 3â4 band. The depth of explanation supports a topâofâband score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a clear research question focused on the axial variation of the magnetic field of a single flat coil and immediately embeds it within a concrete experimental context (teslameter probe positions, constant current).  Background theory (AmpÃ¨re, Biot-Savart) and justification of interest are presented before the question, demonstrating a specific and appropriate setting rather than a broad overview.  Minor language errors do not impede understanding, so the work best fits the top band; a score of 5 (rather than 6) is assigned because the everyday relevance of the investigation is only briefly touched upon.

Instrument (systematic) uncertainties are quoted; random uncertainty is estimated via half-range; error bars are shown; percentage errors are calculated.  However, propagation of uncertainty to derived quantities (e.g., polynomial fit peak) is absent, and the gradient uncertainty uses doubtful figures.  Consideration is therefore present but with significant omissions, fitting the 3-4 band; 4 is assigned.

Independent, dependent and controlled variables are tabulated, non-controlled factors are identified, and preliminary trials plus risk assessment are described.  This shows that the student has thought about collecting *relevant and sufficient* data.  However, the explanations of why each control is critical (e.g., coil alignment, environmental field) are brief, and the choice of probe range (0â24 cm) is not justified quantitatively.  The level therefore aligns with the 3-4 band; a top-of-band score of 4 is merited.

A table lists four specific weaknesses (probe positioning, current stability, environmental fields, teslameter sensitivity) and briefly states their influence on results.  The relative severity is mentioned qualitatively in two cases but not analysed systematically, so the explanation remains at the âdescribesâ level.  This corresponds to band 3-4 with a score of 3.

The apparatus list includes model types or measurable specifications (teslameter Â±0.005 mT, coil radius 0.065 m, N = 95, current fixed at 3.3 A, probe positions, five repeats).  Step-by-step procedures and a circuit diagram allow a competent peer to reproduce the set-up with little doubt.  A few gaps remain (exact coil thickness, how the probe is centred), so the strand is placed in the 5-6 band but awarded 5, not the full 6.

Averages, half-range uncertainties, theoretical predictions from equation (6), percentage errors and best-fit curves are produced correctly.  Nonetheless, the polynomial fit is unjustified, a spurious linear gradient (0.3098) is later treated as if linearity held, and some rounding in the processed table is inconsistent.  Processing quality is therefore âsome significant omissions/inaccuraciesâ, giving band 3-4; 4 is given to acknowledge generally competent work.

For each weakness the student proposes a realistic remedy (higher-resolution probe, better rheostat, isolated laboratory, improved teslameter).  The suggestions are relevant but not fully *explained* in terms of how much improvement would be expected or how to implement them.  Thus the strand lies in the 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

Data tables are complete, units and uncertainties are stated, processed results are placed in a second table, and graphs include error bars and trendlines.  Despite some formatting glitches, the recording is *both clear and precise* overall.  Minor issues (mis-aligned headings, French decimal commas) prevent the perfect score, so 5 is awarded within the top band.

The student compares experimental and theoretical curves, quotes percentage errors for each point, and comments on overall trend alignment.  Yet the analysis stops at descriptive statements; no deeper physics (e.g., edge-field corrections, coil finite thickness) is invoked to justify differences.  This meets âsome relevant comparisonâ, earning 3 in the 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates that the magnetic field increases then decreases along the axis and asserts qualitative agreement with Biot-Savart, supported by the plotted data.  Quantitative support (e.g., comparison of peak positions or ÏÂ²) is missing, and the discussion of discrepancies is brief.  Hence the conclusion is âdescribed but not fully consistent/justifiedâ, placing it in the 3-4 band with a lower-band score of 3.

annotations.comment: The gradient uncertainty is calculated correctly (Î=0.0026), showcasing appropriate propagation and clear communication of derived uncertainties.
The experimental error percentages are exceptionally high at some positions (85 % at x=0 cm), suggesting possible systematic calibration bias; address teslameter zero-offset.
The student formulates a clear and focused research question with explicit probe positions and a constant current context, embedding it within a specific experimental framework.
The introduction outlines relevant theory (AmpÃ¨reâs law) but applies the infinite-solenoid formula directly to a flat coil; clarify the assumptions and derivation path for the chosen geometry.
Consider elaborating on the everyday relevance or applications of flat coils to strengthen the context and justify the investigationâs broader importance.
The derivation to equation (2) treats the coil as an infinite solenoid; for a flat coil geometry, reference a BiotâSavart integration or justify the simplification.
The BiotâSavart expression used in the text assumes a single loop at its center; the student should clarify the domain of validity and any approximations for an axial point.
The final theoretical formula (6) is presented without deriving the exponent 3 on the sine term; include or reference the full derivation to improve reproducibility.
The hypothesis accurately predicts a rise then fall of B with x but could be tightened by referring explicitly to the theoretical peak position derived earlier.
Variables are listed, but the choice of 2 cm increments lacks justification; explain why this spatial resolution is sufficient for capturing the field variation.
The variables table clearly identifies independent, dependent, and controlled variables with uncertainties, aiding reproducibility and clarity.
The control for coil alignment is described but not operationalized; detail how the probe centering is checked at each position to minimize misalignment.
The apparatus list omits key specifications (e.g., coil thickness, manufacturer details); include full model numbers or dimensions for reproducibility.
The step-by-step procedure is clear, sequential, and includes multiple repeats, facilitating reproducibility.
Preliminary trials are described but lack quantitative results to justify the chosen current and range; summarize initial data to support experimental settings.
The raw data table is comprehensive, includes uncertainties, and labels units clearly, ensuring precise recording of measurements.
Random uncertainty is estimated via half-range, but propagation through derived quantities (e.g., fit peak position) is missing; include uncertainty propagation for key results.
The processed-data table correctly presents means and uncertainties, demonstrating accurate data processing.
Some processed values (e.g., 3.024 mT) use inconsistent significant figures; standardize rounding rules to reflect measurement precision.
The polynomial regression degree 6 is not justified and risks overfitting; discuss why a high-order fit is appropriate for the data trend.
The theoretical results table includes uncertainties for each position, demonstrating rigorous calculation of systematic errors.
The theoretical graph analysis conflates experimental behavior with theoretical trends; separate discussion of each dataset to avoid confusion.
The conclusion restates qualitative agreement but lacks quantitative comparison (e.g., peak positions or percentage deviation); include hard numbers to reinforce support.
Suggest adding a brief quantitative summary (e.g., maximum field values and their deviations) in the conclusion to strengthen consistency with analysis.
Weaknesses table lists specific sources but does not explain their relative impact; quantify or rank limitations to guide prioritization of improvements.
The strengths table partially repeats procedural points, and the third point is truncated; ensure each strength is fully articulated and linked to its impact.
The proposed extension to Helmholtz coils is well justified and shows insight into further exploring field uniformity and comparative analysis.
The bibliography contains multiple URLs but lacks a consistent citation style or DOIs; standardize references to improve traceability and academic rigor.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

similarExemplars.title: To what extent does the number of turns in a coil (50, 100, 150, 200) affect the strength of the magnetic field it produces, when varying voltages (1.5V, 3V, 4.5V, 6V, 9V, 12V) are applied across it?

userSubmission.researchQuestion: To what extent does the number of turns in a coil (50, 100, 150, 200) affect the strength of the magnetic field it produces, when varying voltages (1.5V, 3V, 4.5V, 6V, 9V, 12V) are applied across it?
